Made with MyST

Variational Method

What you will learn

  • Why we need approximations: The Schrodinger equation can be solved analytically only for a handful of simple systems. The hydrogen atom is the most complex atomic case with a closed-form solution; helium and any multi-electron system are intractable.

  • The variational theorem: For any trial wavefunction, the computed energy is always greater than or equal to the true ground-state energy. This gives a rigorous upper bound and a way to rank approximations.

  • Trial functions and parameters: Start from an educated guess for the wavefunction, then tune its parameters to minimize the energy and approach the exact value.

  • Worked examples: Particle in a box, the hydrogen atom with Gaussian trial functions, and the helium atom with an effective nuclear charge.

  • The linear variational method: Expanding the trial function in a basis set turns a hard differential-equation problem into a tractable linear algebra problem, where we solve for eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix. This idea underlies electronic-structure methods such as Hartree-Fock.

Why approximations are needed¶

  • The Schrodinger equation can be solved analytically only for simple systems.

  • For atomic systems, the hydrogen atom represents the most complex case with an analytical solution.

  • For systems with multiple interacting electrons, such as the helium atom or other multi-electron systems, analytical solutions become intractable.

  • Approximation methods are therefore essential for finding solutions to complex quantum systems. These methods let us estimate solutions and evaluate how close the approximate results are to the true values.

The variational method provides a systematic approach for making approximations and a quantitative way to assess the convergence of predictions toward exact values. Its core idea is simple:

  • Begin with an educated guess by selecting a trial function to represent the wavefunction of the system.

  • Adjust the parameters of the trial function to minimize the energy, bringing the solution closer to the exact value.

Variational theorem¶

  • The variational method states that for any trial (approximate) function ∣ϕ⟩|\phi\rangle∣ϕ⟩, the computed energy always comes out greater than or equal to the exact (true) ground-state energy.

Variational Theorem of Quantum Mechanics
Eϕ=⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩≥E0E_{\phi}=\frac{\langle \phi \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi\rangle}{\langle \phi \mid \phi\rangle} \geq E_0Eϕ​=⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩​≥E0​
(1)

  • H^\hat{H}H^ is the Hamiltonian of the problem we want to solve.

  • ∣ϕ⟩|\phi\rangle∣ϕ⟩ is a trial wavefunction with unknown parameters we want to determine.

  • E0E_0E0​ is the true ground-state energy, which is generally not known to us.

  • When the trial wavefunction is not normalized, we divide by ⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩\langle \phi \mid \phi\rangle⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩. The expression simplifies when the trial function is normalized beforehand, so that ⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩=1\langle \phi \mid \phi\rangle=1⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩=1.

  • If the true ground-state wavefunction ψ0\psi_0ψ0​ is inserted in place of the trial function, the equality is reached. For all other (trial) wavefunctions the energy expectation value on the left side will always be larger. The ratio is also called the Rayleigh ratio.

Consequences of the variational theorem¶

  1. Ground-state energy is the lowest possible energy for the system.

  2. By minimizing the energy functional we obtain the most accurate prediction for a given trial function.

  3. More parameters give us more handles to vary, and hence more accurate solutions.

Worked examples¶

Example: Particle in a box

Consider the one-dimensional particle in a box (boundaries at 0 and aaa). Use the variational theorem to obtain an upper bound for the ground-state energy using the following normalized trial wavefunction:

ψt(x)=30a5/2x(a−x)\psi_t(x) = \frac{\sqrt{30}}{a^{5/2}}x(a - x)ψt​(x)=a5/230​​x(a−x)
(2)
Solution:

Clearly this is not the correct ground-state wavefunction. First we check that it satisfies the boundary conditions: ψt(0)=0\psi_t(0) = 0ψt​(0)=0 and ψt(a)=0\psi_t(a) = 0ψt​(a)=0 (OK). The Hamiltonian for this problem is:

H^=−ℏ22md2dx2,0≤x≤a\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2}{dx^2}, \quad 0\le x\le aH^=−2mℏ2​dx2d2​,0≤x≤a
(3)

Plugging both the Hamiltonian and ψt\psi_tψt​ into the energy expression gives:

∫0aψt∗H^ψt dτ=−30ℏ22a5m∫0a(ax−x2)d2dx2(ax−x2)dx\int_0^a\psi_t^*\hat{H}\psi_t \, d\tau = -\frac{30\hbar^2}{2a^5m}\int_0^a\left( ax - x^2\right)\frac{d^2}{dx^2}\left( ax - x^2\right)dx∫0a​ψt∗​H^ψt​dτ=−2a5m30ℏ2​∫0a​(ax−x2)dx2d2​(ax−x2)dx
(4)
=30ℏ2a5m∫0a(ax−x2)dx=5ℏ2a2m≥E1= \frac{30\hbar^2}{a^5m}\int_0^a\left( ax-x^2\right) dx = \frac{5\hbar^2}{a^2m}\ge E_1=a5m30ℏ2​∫0a​(ax−x2)dx=a2m5ℏ2​≥E1​
(5)

As indicated, this gives an upper limit for the ground-state energy E1E_1E1​.

Example: Hydrogen atom with a Gaussian trial function

  • The exact solution for the ground state is ψ(r)=1(πa03)1/2e−r/a0\psi(r)=\frac{1}{(\pi a^3_0)^{1/2}}e^{-r/a_0}ψ(r)=(πa03​)1/21​e−r/a0​ with E1=−0.5 hE_1 = -0.5 \, hE1​=−0.5h (in Hartree atomic units).

  • Use a trial function ϕ=e−αr2\phi=e^{-\alpha r^2}ϕ=e−αr2 to predict the ground-state energy.

Solution:

Plug the trial function into the energy expression, computing the energy as a function of the parameter α\alphaα:

Etrial=⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩E_{trial} = \frac{\langle \phi \mid \hat{H} \mid \phi\rangle}{\langle \phi \mid \phi\rangle}Etrial​=⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩​
(6)
Etrial(α)=4π∫0∞e−αr2[−ℏ22μ∇r2−e24πϵ0r]e−αr2 r2dr4π∫0∞e−2αr2r2drE_{trial}(\alpha) = \frac{4\pi \int^{\infty}_0 e^{-\alpha r^2}\left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2\mu}\nabla_r^2 -\frac{e^2}{4\pi \epsilon_0 r}\right] e^{-\alpha r^2} \, r^2 dr }{4\pi \int^{\infty}_0 e^{-2\alpha r^2} r^2 dr}Etrial​(α)=4π∫0∞​e−2αr2r2dr4π∫0∞​e−αr2[−2μℏ2​∇r2​−4πϵ0​re2​]e−αr2r2dr​
(7)
Etrial(α)=3ℏ2α2me−e2α1/22ϵ0π3/2E_{trial}(\alpha)=\frac{3\hbar^2 \alpha}{2m_e}-\frac{e^2 \alpha^{1/2}}{\sqrt{2}\epsilon_0 \pi^{3/2}}Etrial​(α)=2me​3ℏ2α​−2​ϵ0​π3/2e2α1/2​
(8)

Minimization with respect to the parameter α\alphaα gives the best value of the energy:

∂Etrial(α)∂α=0\frac{\partial E_{trial}(\alpha)}{\partial \alpha} = 0∂α∂Etrial​(α)​=0
(9)
αmin=mee418π3ϵ02ℏ4\alpha_{min} = \frac{m_e e^4}{18 \pi^3 \epsilon^2_0 \hbar^4}αmin​=18π3ϵ02​ℏ4me​e4​
(10)

Finally, we plug this parameter back into the energy function to obtain the energy minimized with respect to α\alphaα, that is E(αmin)E(\alpha_{min})E(αmin​):

E0=−0.5 h,Etrial(αmin)=−0.424E_0 = -0.5 \, h, \qquad E_{trial}(\alpha_{min}) = -0.424E0​=−0.5h,Etrial​(αmin​)=−0.424
(11)

This is about 15% error. Not too bad for a start. Adding more parameters and functions will reduce the error.

The helium atom is tough¶

  • The Schrodinger equation for the helium atom is already extremely complicated mathematically. No analytic solution to this equation has been found. However, with certain approximations, useful results can be obtained. The Hamiltonian for the He atom can be written as:

H^=−ℏ22me(Δ1+Δ2)⏟Kinetic energy−14πϵ0(Ze2r1+Ze2r2−e2r12⏞Tough!)⏟Potential energy{\hat{H} = \underbrace{-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\left(\Delta_1 + \Delta_2\right)}_{\textnormal{Kinetic energy}} \underbrace{- \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\left(\frac{Ze^2}{r_1} + \frac{Ze^2}{r_2} \overbrace{- \frac{e^2}{r_{12}}}^{\textnormal{Tough!}}\right)}_{\textnormal{Potential energy}}}H^=Kinetic energy−2me​ℏ2​(Δ1​+Δ2​)​​Potential energy−4πϵ0​1​⎝⎛​r1​Ze2​+r2​Ze2​−r12​e2​​Tough!​⎠⎞​​​
(12)

  • Here Δ1\Delta_1Δ1​ is the Laplacian for the coordinates of electron 1 and Δ2\Delta_2Δ2​ for electron 2; r1r_1r1​ is the distance of electron 1 from the nucleus, r2r_2r2​ the distance of electron 2 from the nucleus, and r12r_{12}r12​ the distance between electrons 1 and 2. For the He atom Z=2Z = 2Z=2.

Independent-electron approximation¶

  • Ignore the tough term containing r12r_{12}r12​. In this case the Hamiltonian becomes a sum of two hydrogen-like atoms:

H^=H^1+H^2{\hat{H} = \hat{H}_1 + \hat{H}_2}H^=H^1​+H^2​
(13)
H^1=−ℏ22meΔ1−Ze24πϵ0r1{\hat{H}_1 = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\Delta_1 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0r_1}}H^1​=−2me​ℏ2​Δ1​−4πϵ0​r1​Ze2​
(14)
H^2=−ℏ22meΔ2−Ze24πϵ0r2{\hat{H}_2 = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\Delta_2 - \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0r_2}}H^2​=−2me​ℏ2​Δ2​−4πϵ0​r2​Ze2​
(15)

  • Because the Hamiltonian is a sum of two independent parts, the Schrodinger equation separates into two (each a hydrogen-like atom equation):

H^1ψ(r1)=E1ψ(r1){\hat{H}_1\psi(r_1) = E_1\psi(r_1)}H^1​ψ(r1​)=E1​ψ(r1​)
(16)
H^2ψ(r2)=E2ψ(r2){\hat{H}_2\psi(r_2) = E_2\psi(r_2)}H^2​ψ(r2​)=E2​ψ(r2​)
(17)

The total energy is a sum of E1E_1E1​ and E2E_2E2​, and the total wavefunction is a product of ψ(r1)\psi(r_1)ψ(r1​) and ψ(r2)\psi(r_2)ψ(r2​). Based on our previous wavefunction table for hydrogen-like atoms:

E=E1+E2=−RZ2(1n12+1n22){E = E_1 + E_2 = -RZ^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2} + \frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)}E=E1​+E2​=−RZ2(n12​1​+n22​1​)
(18)
ψ(r1)ψ(r2)=1π(Za0)3/2e−Zr1/a01π(Za0)3/2e−Zr2/a0=1π(Za0)3e−Z(r1+r2)/a0{\psi(r_1)\psi(r_2) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-Zr_1/a_0}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi}}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^{3/2}e^{-Zr_2/a_0} =\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\frac{Z}{a_0}\right)^3e^{-Z(r_1 + r_2)/a_0}}ψ(r1​)ψ(r2​)=π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2e−Zr1​/a0​π​1​(a0​Z​)3/2e−Zr2​/a0​=π1​(a0​Z​)3e−Z(r1​+r2​)/a0​
(19)

Consequences of the independent-electron approximation¶

  • For a ground-state He atom both electrons reside in the lowest-energy orbital, so the total wavefunction is ψ(r1,r2)=ψ(r1)ψ(r2)=ψ(1)ψ(2)=1s(1)1s(2)\psi(r_1,r_2) = \psi(r_1)\psi(r_2) = \psi(1)\psi(2) = 1s(1)1s(2)ψ(r1​,r2​)=ψ(r1​)ψ(r2​)=ψ(1)ψ(2)=1s(1)1s(2). The energy from this approximation is not sufficiently accurate (it misses electron-electron repulsion), but the wavefunction is useful for qualitative analysis. The variational principle gives a systematic way to assess how good our approximation is.

  • The exact ground-state energy has been found (by very extensive analytic and numerical calculations) to be -79.0 eV. Using the approximate wavefunction, we can calculate the expectation value of the energy. This yields -74.8 eV, so the error in energy for this wavefunction is 5.2 eV. Note that the approximate value is, in accordance with the variational principle, higher than the true energy.

A better approximation¶

  • We take the wavefunction from the previous step and use the nuclear charge ZZZ as a variational parameter. The variational principle states that minimizing the energy expectation value with respect to ZZZ should approach the true value from above (but will not reach it).

  • The obtained value of ZZZ is less than the true ZZZ (= 2). This can be understood in terms of electrons shielding the nucleus from each other, giving a reduced effective nuclear charge.

E=⟨ψ∣H^∣ψ⟩=...=[Z2−27Z8]e24πϵ0a0{E = \langle\psi |\hat{H}|\psi\rangle = ... = \left[ Z^2 - \frac{27Z}{8}\right]\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0a_0}}E=⟨ψ∣H^∣ψ⟩=...=[Z2−827Z​]4πϵ0​a0​e2​
(20)

  • To minimize this expression we differentiate with respect to ZZZ and set it to zero (the extremum here is clearly a minimum):

dEdZ=(2Z−278)e24πϵ0a0=0{\frac{dE}{dZ} = \left(2Z - \frac{27}{8}\right)\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0a_0} = 0}dZdE​=(2Z−827​)4πϵ0​a0​e2​=0
(21)

  • This gives Z=27/16≈1.7Z = 27/16 \approx 1.7Z=27/16≈1.7 and E≈−77.5E \approx -77.5E≈−77.5 eV (compared with -74.8 eV before and -79.0 eV exact). This result could be improved by adding more terms and variables to the trial wavefunction. For example, higher hydrogen-like orbitals with appropriate variational coefficients would yield a much better result.

The linear variational method¶

  • How does the variational method scale to harder problems in practice? If we cannot solve for ψ\psiψ exactly, we can expand the wavefunction in a convenient basis set fnf_nfn​ (a set of functions indexed by nnn, like a Fourier series) and tune the expansion to get as close as possible to the true energy and wavefunction.

ϕ(r)=∑nNcnfn(r)\phi(r) = \sum_n^N c_nf_n(r)ϕ(r)=n∑N​cn​fn​(r)
(22)

  • With an infinite number of basis functions we could in principle approximate any function and obtain a nearly exact numerical solution. Computationally this is not feasible, so we truncate the expansion to a finite number NNN of basis functions and minimize the energy with respect to the coefficients cnc_ncn​.

  • Using a linear combination of trial functions transforms a difficult quantum-mechanics problem into a more tractable linear algebra task: instead of solving differential equations for eigenfunctions and eigenvalues, we solve for the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a matrix.

A two-function example¶

  • We illustrate the idea and the general matrix construction with a simple example of two basis functions (N=2N=2N=2):

ϕ=c1f1+c2f2\phi = c_1f_1 + c_2f_2ϕ=c1​f1​+c2​f2​
(23)

  • There is no need to define these functions explicitly yet, so we leave them as generic functions f1f_1f1​ and f2f_2f2​. The energy is:

Eϕ=⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩=⟨c1f1+c2f2∣H^∣c1f1+c2f2⟩⟨c1f1+c2f2∣c1f1+c2f2⟩E_\phi = \frac{\langle\phi|\hat{H}|\phi\rangle}{\langle\phi|\phi\rangle} = \frac{\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|\hat{H}|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle}{\langle c_1f_1 + c_2f_2|c_1f_1 + c_2f_2 \rangle}Eϕ​=⟨ϕ∣ϕ⟩⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩​=⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩⟨c1​f1​+c2​f2​∣H^∣c1​f1​+c2​f2​⟩​
(24)

  • Expanding the brackets, the numerator and denominator are built from two kinds of matrix elements, the Hamiltonian matrix elements HijH_{ij}Hij​ and the overlap matrix elements SijS_{ij}Sij​:

Hij=⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩,Sij=⟨fi∣fj⟩H_{ij} = \langle f_i|\hat{H}|f_j\rangle, \qquad S_{ij} = \langle f_i|f_j\rangleHij​=⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩,Sij​=⟨fi​∣fj​⟩
(25)

so that

Eϕ=c12H11+2c1c2H12+c22H22c12S11+2c1c2S12+c22S22E_\phi = \frac{c_1^2 H_{11} + 2 c_1 c_2 H_{12} + c_2^2 H_{22}}{c_1^2 S_{11} + 2 c_1 c_2 S_{12} + c_2^2 S_{22}}Eϕ​=c12​S11​+2c1​c2​S12​+c22​S22​c12​H11​+2c1​c2​H12​+c22​H22​​
(26)

Minimization leads to a generalized eigenvalue problem¶

  • Since Eϕ≥E0E_\phi \geq E_0Eϕ​≥E0​ for any trial function ϕ\phiϕ, we minimize EϕE_\phiEϕ​ by varying the parameters c1c_1c1​ and c2c_2c2​.

  • Minimizing with respect to c1c_1c1​ means differentiating EϕE_\phiEϕ​ with respect to c1c_1c1​ and setting the derivative to zero:

∂Eϕ∂c1=0=c1(H11−ES11)+c2(H12−ES12)\frac{\partial E_\phi}{\partial c_1} = 0 = c_1(H_{11} - ES_{11}) + c_2(H_{12} - ES_{12})∂c1​∂Eϕ​​=0=c1​(H11​−ES11​)+c2​(H12​−ES12​)
(27)

  • Minimizing with respect to c2c_2c2​ gives:

∂Eϕ∂c2=0=c1(H12−ES12)+c2(H22−ES22)\frac{\partial E_\phi}{\partial c_2} = 0 = c_1(H_{12} - ES_{12}) + c_2(H_{22} - ES_{22})∂c2​∂Eϕ​​=0=c1​(H12​−ES12​)+c2​(H22​−ES22​)
(28)

  • These two coupled linear equations can be written compactly as a matrix equation:

Linear variational (generalized eigenvalue) problem
Hc=E Sc\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\,\mathbf{S}\mathbf{c}Hc=ESc
(29)
[H11H12H12H22][c1c2]=E[S11S12S12S22][c1c2]\begin{bmatrix} H_{11} & H_{12} \\ H_{12} & H_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix} = E \begin{bmatrix} S_{11} & S_{12} \\ S_{12} & S_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_1 \\ c_2 \end{bmatrix}[H11​H12​​H12​H22​​][c1​c2​​]=E[S11​S12​​S12​S22​​][c1​c2​​]
(30)

  • H\mathbf{H}H is the Hamiltonian matrix with elements Hij=⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩H_{ij} = \langle f_i|\hat{H}|f_j\rangleHij​=⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩.

  • S\mathbf{S}S is the overlap matrix with elements Sij=⟨fi∣fj⟩S_{ij} = \langle f_i|f_j\rangleSij​=⟨fi​∣fj​⟩.

  • The lowest eigenvalue EEE is the variational estimate of the ground-state energy; the corresponding eigenvector c\mathbf{c}c gives the best coefficients.

  • A nontrivial solution exists only when the secular determinant vanishes, det⁡(H−E S)=0\det(\mathbf{H} - E\,\mathbf{S}) = 0det(H−ES)=0. Solving this determinant gives the variational energies. For an orthonormal basis (S=I\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I}S=I) this reduces to the ordinary eigenvalue problem Hc=Ec\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\mathbf{c}Hc=Ec.

Worked example: particle in a box by linear variation

Consider a free particle in 1D bound to 0≤x≤a0\leq x\leq a0≤x≤a, with Hamiltonian H^=−ℏ22md2dx2\hat{H} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2}{dx^2}H^=−2mℏ2​dx2d2​. Although we can solve this analytically, it is instructive to see the variational solution. We approximate ψ(x)\psi(x)ψ(x) as an expansion in two basis functions f1=x(a−x)f_1=x(a-x)f1​=x(a−x) and f2=x2(a−x)2f_2=x^2(a-x)^2f2​=x2(a−x)2:

ψ(x)≈c1x(a−x)+c2x2(a−x)2\psi(x) \approx c_1x(a-x) + c_2x^2(a-x)^2ψ(x)≈c1​x(a−x)+c2​x2(a−x)2
(31)

Computing the matrix elements (by integrating each ⟨fi∣H^∣fj⟩\langle f_i|\hat{H}|f_j\rangle⟨fi​∣H^∣fj​⟩ and ⟨fi∣fj⟩\langle f_i|f_j\rangle⟨fi​∣fj​⟩ over 0≤x≤a0\le x\le a0≤x≤a) gives:

H=ℏ2a3m[16a230a230a4105],S=a510[13a214a214a463]\mathbf{H} = \frac{\hbar^2a^3}{m} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{6} & \frac{a^2}{30}\\ \frac{a^2}{30} & \frac{a^4}{105} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{S} = \frac{a^5}{10} \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{3} & \frac{a^2}{14}\\ \frac{a^2}{14} & \frac{a^4}{63} \end{bmatrix}H=mℏ2a3​[61​30a2​​30a2​105a4​​],S=10a5​[31​14a2​​14a2​63a4​​]
(32)

Solving the generalized eigenvalue problem (with a=ℏ=m=1a = \hbar = m = 1a=ℏ=m=1) gives a lowest energy of

Eϕ=4.9349 ℏ2mE_\phi = 4.9349 \, \frac{\hbar^2}{m}Eϕ​=4.9349mℏ2​
(33)

which is essentially the exact analytic value E1=π2ℏ22≈4.9348 ℏ2mE_1 = \frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2} \approx 4.9348 \, \frac{\hbar^2}{m}E1​=2π2ℏ2​≈4.9348mℏ2​. The variational solution captures both the energy and the shape of the ground-state wavefunction. See the demo notebooks at the end of this chapter for the full calculation and plots.

Problems¶

Problem 1¶

Problem 1: Hydrogen atom by variation

Use the variational principle to obtain the lowest-energy solution to the hydrogen atom Schrodinger equation in spherical coordinates using the following trial wavefunctions:

  • (a) ψtrial=e−kr\psi_{trial} = e^{-kr}ψtrial​=e−kr with kkk as a variational parameter.

  • (b) ψtrial=e−kr2\psi_{trial} = e^{-kr^2}ψtrial​=e−kr2 with kkk as a variational parameter.

  • (c) Which trial function gives the better (lower) energy?

Both trial functions depend only on rrr, so the angular terms drop out of the Laplacian. You may find the following integrals useful:

∫0∞xne−axdx=n!an+1,∫0∞xme−ax2dx=Γ[(m+1)/2]2a(m+1)/2\int_0^\infty x^ne^{-ax}dx = \frac{n!}{a^{n+1}}, \qquad \int_0^\infty x^me^{-ax^2}dx = \frac{\Gamma[(m + 1)/2]}{2a^{(m + 1) / 2}}∫0∞​xne−axdx=an+1n!​,∫0∞​xme−ax2dx=2a(m+1)/2Γ[(m+1)/2]​
(34)
Γ[n+1]=n!,Γ[n+1]=nΓ[n],Γ[12]=π\Gamma[n + 1] = n!, \qquad \Gamma[n + 1] = n\Gamma[n], \qquad \Gamma\left[\frac{1}{2}\right] = \sqrt{\pi}Γ[n+1]=n!,Γ[n+1]=nΓ[n],Γ[21​]=π​
(35)
Solution:

Use the variational principle and employ spherical coordinates in the integrations:

E=∫ψtrial∗Hψtrialdτ∫ψtrial∗ψtrialdτE = \frac{\int\psi^*_{trial}H\psi_{trial}d\tau}{\int\psi^*_{trial}\psi_{trial}d\tau}E=∫ψtrial∗​ψtrial​dτ∫ψtrial∗​Hψtrial​dτ​
(36)

(a) Normalization integral:

∫ψtrial∗ψtrialdτ=∫0∞e−2kr4πr2dr⏟dτ=4π∫0∞r2e−2krdr=πk3\int\psi^*_{trial}\psi_{trial}d\tau = \int_0^\infty e^{-2kr} \underbrace{4\pi r^2dr}_{d\tau} = 4\pi\int_0^\infty r^2e^{-2kr}dr = \frac{\pi}{k^3}∫ψtrial∗​ψtrial​dτ=∫0∞​e−2krdτ4πr2dr​​=4π∫0∞​r2e−2krdr=k3π​
(37)

Energy numerator:

∫ψtrial∗Hψtrialdτ=∫(e−kr)(−ℏ22meΔ−e24πϵ0r)(e−kr)dτ\int\psi_{trial}^*H\psi_{trial}d\tau = \int\left( e^{-kr}\right) \left( -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\Delta - \frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0r}\right) \left( e^{-kr}\right)d\tau∫ψtrial∗​Hψtrial​dτ=∫(e−kr)(−2me​ℏ2​Δ−4πϵ0​re2​)(e−kr)dτ
(38)
=−4πℏ22me∫0∞e−2kr(r2k2−2kr)dr−e2ϵ0∫0∞re−2krdr= -\frac{4\pi\hbar^2}{2m_e}\int_0^\infty e^{-2kr}(r^2k^2 - 2kr)dr - \frac{e^2}{\epsilon_0}\int_0^\infty re^{-2kr}dr=−2me​4πℏ2​∫0∞​e−2kr(r2k2−2kr)dr−ϵ0​e2​∫0∞​re−2krdr
(39)
=−πℏ22mek+πℏ2mek−e24k2ϵ0= -\frac{\pi\hbar^2}{2m_ek} + \frac{\pi\hbar^2}{m_ek} - \frac{e^2}{4k^2\epsilon_0}=−2me​kπℏ2​+me​kπℏ2​−4k2ϵ0​e2​
(40)

Dividing numerator by denominator:

E=ℏ2k22me−e2k4πϵ0E = \frac{\hbar^2k^2}{2m_e} - \frac{e^2k}{4\pi\epsilon_0}E=2me​ℏ2k2​−4πϵ0​e2k​
(41)

Minimize with respect to kkk:

dEdk=ℏ2kme−e24πϵ0=0⇒k=mee24πϵ0ℏ2⇒E=−12mee4(4ϵ0)2ℏ2=−hcR\frac{dE}{dk} = \frac{\hbar^2k}{m_e} - \frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 0 \Rightarrow k = \frac{m_ee^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0\hbar^2} \Rightarrow E = -\frac{1}{2}\frac{m_ee^4}{(4\epsilon_0)^2\hbar^2} = -hcRdkdE​=me​ℏ2k​−4πϵ0​e2​=0⇒k=4πϵ0​ℏ2me​e2​⇒E=−21​(4ϵ0​)2ℏ2me​e4​=−hcR
(42)

where RRR is the Rydberg constant. This trial function recovers the exact ground-state energy because it has the correct functional form.

(b) The logic is identical. The normalization integral is

∫ψtrial∗ψtrialdτ=4πΓ[3/2]2(2k)3/2=π3/2(2k)3/2\int\psi^*_{trial}\psi_{trial}d\tau = \frac{4\pi\Gamma[3/2]}{2(2k)^{3/2}} = \frac{\pi^{3/2}}{(2k)^{3/2}}∫ψtrial∗​ψtrial​dτ=2(2k)3/24πΓ[3/2]​=(2k)3/2π3/2​
(43)

The Laplacian of the Gaussian is

Δψtrial=(4k2r2−6k)e−kr2\Delta\psi_{trial} = \left( 4k^2r^2 - 6k\right) e^{-kr^2}Δψtrial​=(4k2r2−6k)e−kr2
(44)

Carrying out the integrals gives the energy

E=3ℏ2k2me−e2k2π3/2ϵ0E = \frac{3\hbar^2k}{2m_e} - \frac{e^2\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{2}\pi^{3/2}\epsilon_0}E=2me​3ℏ2k​−2​π3/2ϵ0​e2k​​
(45)

Minimizing,

∂E∂k=3ℏ22me−e222π3/2ϵ0k=0⇒k=me2e418π3ϵ02ℏ4\frac{\partial E}{\partial k} = \frac{3\hbar^2}{2m_e} - \frac{e^2}{2\sqrt{2}\pi^{3/2}\epsilon_0\sqrt{k}} = 0 \Rightarrow k = \frac{m_e^2e^4}{18\pi^3\epsilon_0^2\hbar^4}∂k∂E​=2me​3ℏ2​−22​π3/2ϵ0​k​e2​=0⇒k=18π3ϵ02​ℏ4me2​e4​
(46)

and the total energy at this point is

E=−mee412π3ϵ02ℏ2=−83πhcR≈−0.85 hcRE = -\frac{m_ee^4}{12\pi^3\epsilon_0^2\hbar^2} = -\frac{8}{3\pi}hcR \approx -0.85\,hcRE=−12π3ϵ02​ℏ2me​e4​=−3π8​hcR≈−0.85hcR
(47)

(c) The function in (a) gives the lower energy (−1.0 hcR-1.0\,hcR−1.0hcR versus −0.85 hcR-0.85\,hcR−0.85hcR) and is therefore the better trial function. The exponential has the correct cusp at the origin, while the Gaussian does not.

Problem 2¶

Problem 2: Why is the bound always from above?

Expand an arbitrary normalized trial function in the exact eigenbasis of H^\hat{H}H^, ∣ϕ⟩=∑nan∣ψn⟩|\phi\rangle = \sum_n a_n |\psi_n\rangle∣ϕ⟩=∑n​an​∣ψn​⟩ with H^∣ψn⟩=En∣ψn⟩\hat{H}|\psi_n\rangle = E_n|\psi_n\rangleH^∣ψn​⟩=En​∣ψn​⟩ and E0≤E1≤E2≤…E_0 \le E_1 \le E_2 \le \dotsE0​≤E1​≤E2​≤…. Show that ⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩=∑n∣an∣2En≥E0\langle \phi|\hat{H}|\phi\rangle = \sum_n |a_n|^2 E_n \ge E_0⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩=∑n​∣an​∣2En​≥E0​, and state the condition under which equality holds.

Solution:

Because the ∣ψn⟩|\psi_n\rangle∣ψn​⟩ are orthonormal,

⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩=∑n,mam∗an⟨ψm∣H^∣ψn⟩=∑n,mam∗anEnδmn=∑n∣an∣2En.\langle \phi|\hat{H}|\phi\rangle = \sum_{n,m} a_m^* a_n \langle \psi_m|\hat{H}|\psi_n\rangle = \sum_{n,m} a_m^* a_n E_n \delta_{mn} = \sum_n |a_n|^2 E_n.⟨ϕ∣H^∣ϕ⟩=n,m∑​am∗​an​⟨ψm​∣H^∣ψn​⟩=n,m∑​am∗​an​En​δmn​=n∑​∣an​∣2En​.
(48)

Normalization gives ∑n∣an∣2=1\sum_n |a_n|^2 = 1∑n​∣an​∣2=1. Since every En≥E0E_n \ge E_0En​≥E0​,

∑n∣an∣2En≥∑n∣an∣2E0=E0.\sum_n |a_n|^2 E_n \ge \sum_n |a_n|^2 E_0 = E_0.n∑​∣an​∣2En​≥n∑​∣an​∣2E0​=E0​.
(49)

Equality holds only when all the weight is on the ground state, ∣a0∣2=1|a_0|^2 = 1∣a0​∣2=1 and an≠0=0a_{n\neq 0} = 0an=0​=0, that is when the trial function equals the exact ground-state wavefunction.

Problem 3¶

Problem 3: Effective nuclear charge of helium

Using the helium trial energy E(Z)=[Z2−27Z8]e24πϵ0a0E(Z) = \left[ Z^2 - \frac{27Z}{8}\right]\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0a_0}E(Z)=[Z2−827Z​]4πϵ0​a0​e2​, confirm that the optimal effective nuclear charge is Z=27/16Z = 27/16Z=27/16 and explain physically why Z<2Z < 2Z<2.

Solution:

Set the derivative to zero:

dEdZ=(2Z−278)e24πϵ0a0=0⇒Z=2716≈1.69.\frac{dE}{dZ} = \left(2Z - \frac{27}{8}\right)\frac{e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0a_0} = 0 \Rightarrow Z = \frac{27}{16} \approx 1.69.dZdE​=(2Z−827​)4πϵ0​a0​e2​=0⇒Z=1627​≈1.69.
(50)

The effective charge is less than the true Z=2Z = 2Z=2 because each electron partially screens the nucleus from the other. From the viewpoint of one electron, the nuclear charge appears reduced by the average presence of the second electron, so the optimal variational charge is smaller than the bare value.

Problem 4¶

Problem 4: Linear variation with an orthonormal basis

In the linear variational method, the trial energy satisfies Hc=E Sc\mathbf{H}\mathbf{c} = E\,\mathbf{S}\mathbf{c}Hc=ESc. Show that if the basis functions are orthonormal (S=I\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{I}S=I), this reduces to an ordinary eigenvalue problem, and explain what the eigenvalues and eigenvectors represent.

Problem 5¶

Problem 5: Secular determinant for two states

For a two-function basis with H11=αH_{11} = \alphaH11​=α, H22=αH_{22} = \alphaH22​=α, H12=βH_{12} = \betaH12​=β, and an orthonormal basis (S11=S22=1S_{11} = S_{22} = 1S11​=S22​=1, S12=0S_{12} = 0S12​=0), solve the secular determinant det⁡(H−EI)=0\det(\mathbf{H} - E\mathbf{I}) = 0det(H−EI)=0 and show that the two variational energies are E±=α±βE_\pm = \alpha \pm \betaE±​=α±β. (This is the bonding and antibonding splitting of two identical atomic orbitals.)
